content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 19, 2008

Lee Brand
Councilmember Elect for District Six
City of Fresno
2600 Fresno Street, Room 2031
Fresno, CA 93721-3602

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-08-178

Dear Mr. Brand:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your question seeks general guidance and is not limited to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTION

As a city councilmember and the majority owner of a property management business, may you make, participate in making, or influence governmental decisions involving a managed property receiving federal rental subsidies, (2) eminent domain

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

File No. I-08-178
Page No. 2

proceedings related to a managed property, or (3) a redevelopment area in which a
managed property is located?

CONCLUSION

Because we do not have sufficient information to determine whether a particular
governmental decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
your economic interests, we are unable to determine whether you have a conflict of
interest in these decisions. However, based on the facts you have provided, you may not
make, participate in making, or influence decisions that have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on Westco Equities, Westco Construction, certain clients or
customers of these businesses, or your personal finances.

FACTS

You were elected to serve on the Fresno City Council for District Six and will be
taking office in January of 2009. As a councilmember, you will also be serving on the
board for Fresno's redevelopment agency.

In addition, you are the president of Westco Equities Inc., a privately held
California corporation, and have a 60 percent ownership interest in this business. Westco
Equities provides property management services to residential and commercial properties
located in Central California. You also have a 60 percent ownership interest in Westco
Construction, Inc., which is a wholly owned subsidiary of Westco Equities. Westco
Construction provides construction services for properties, including those properties
managed by Westco Equities.

Some of the properties managed by Westco Equities are rented to low-income
renters who receive housing assistance in the form of rental subsidies through the Section
8 Rental Voucher Program, a federal housing program. Section 8 is administered
nationally by the United States Department of Housing and Urban Development
("HUD"), and administered locally by the City of Fresno Housing Authority. When
Westco Equities manages the properties, all forms of income, including rents, are placed
in a trust account, as required by the Department of Real Estate. All expenditures,
including payments for management services to Westco Equities, are paid through this
trust account. Section 8 funds make up less than 5 percent of all rents collected by
Westco Equities.

Westco Equities does not own any of the managed properties. Additionally, you
do not have an ownership interest in any of the properties managed by Westco Equities
that receive Section 8 funds or that are located in redevelopment areas. Westco Equities
does not receive any other HUD funds other than those payments to Westco Equities
through the trust account as payments for management services.

File No. I-08-178
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's
economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has
adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an individual has a
disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which we apply to
your question. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

Step One: Are you a "public official?"

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(1).) A "public official" is "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency . . . ."
(Section 82048.) Upon taking office as a member of the Fresno City Council, you will be
a public official within the meaning of the Act.

Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.) You will be making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision when acting in your role as a member of the city council.³

³ If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 ("87200 filers" include members of a city
council) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she
must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as disclosed in Regulation
18702.5(b)(1)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the
duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation 18702.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)

File No. I-08-178
Page No. 4


Step Three: What are your "economic interests?"

A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests. Of the economic interests recognized under the Act, those interests implicated
by your account of the facts are the following:

Real Property -- A public official has an economic interest in real property in
which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b);
Regulation 18703.2.)

Business Entity -- A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in
which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(a) and (d); Regulations 18703.1(a) and (b).)

Sources of Income -- A public official has an economic interest in any source of
income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months
prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) "Income" is defined to
include a pro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official
(or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater
interest. (Section 82030(a).)

Personal Financial Effects -- A public official has an economic interest in his or
her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is known as
the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

Properties Managed by Westco Equities:

Section 82033 of the Act defines "interest in real property" and provides:

"'Interest in real property' includes any leasehold, beneficial or ownership
interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property located in
the jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public
official, or other filer, or his or her immediate family if the fair market
value of the interest is two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. Interests in
real property of an individual includes a pro rata share of interests in real
property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or
immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent
interest or greater."

File No. 1-08-178
Page No. 5


While you have a 60 percent ownership interest in Westco Equities and its wholly
owned subsidiary Westco Construction, you have stated that Westco Equities does not
own any of the properties it manages. Moreover, the mere fact that Westco Equities acts
as a manager for a property does not give rise to an economic interest in real property.⁴

Westco Equities:

You have a 60 percent ownership interest in Westco Equities. From the facts
presented, it appears that you have an investment of $2,000 or more in Westco Equities
and are a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or manager of this business.
Therefore, you have an economic interest in Westco Equities as a business entity.
(Section 87103 (a) and (d).) Moreover, if you have received income of $500 or more
from Westco Equities in the 12 months prior to a governmental decision, you also have
an economic interest in this business as a source of income. (Section 87102(c).)

Westco Construction:

Westco Construction is a wholly owned subsidiary of Westco Equities. Under
Regulation 18703.1(c) and 18703.3(a)(2), an official also has an economic interest in any
business entity which is a parent or subsidiary of, or is otherwise related to, a business in
which the official has an economic interest as either a business entity or a source of
income. Because Westco Construction is a wholly owned subsidiary of Westco Equities,
you also have an economic interest in Westco Construction under both Regulation
18703.1(c) and 18703.3(a)(2). (See Section 18703.1(d) for definition of "parent-
subsidiary relationship" and "otherwise related business entity.")

Clients or Customers of Westco Equities and Westco Construction:

You have a 60 percent interest in both Westco Equities and Westco Construction.
Thus, you have an economic interest in any client or customer of these businesses if your
pro rata share of the income derived from the client or customer aggregates to $500 or
more in the 12 months prior to the governmental decision. (Section 82030(a).) However,
you have not identified any particular clients or customers of Westco Equities and
Westco Construction who may qualify as sources of income. We therefore cannot
analyze any potential conflicts of interest you may have in decisions affecting these
clients or customers. You will still need to determine whether any of the clients or
customers of Westco Equities or Westco Construction qualify as a source of income and
whether a conflict of interest will arise based upon an economic interest in one of these

⁴ Because this letter is based upon the facts presented, our analysis of your potential economic
interests in real property is limited to those properties managed by Westco Equities. If you have other
interests in real property that may be affected by a governmental decision, those interests must also be
considered prior to making, participating in, or influencing the decision. If you need additional assistance
related to other interests in real property, it is advisable that you seek further advice providing all relevant
information.

File No. I-08-178
Page No. 6

clients or customers prior to making, participating in, or influencing any decision that
may affect the client or customer.5

Personal Finances:

A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances.
A governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will
result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her
immediate family increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?

Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a business entity or source of income is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency when that business entity or source of
income, either directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject
of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person."

Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in
governmental decisions under the rules quoted above are regarded as indirectly involved.
(Regulation 18704.1(b).)

You have asked whether you may participate in general discussions related to the
following: (1) decisions involving HUD or the City of Fresno Housing Authority relating
to low income housing, (2) decisions affecting a redevelopment area in which properties
managed by Westco Equities are located, and (3) eminent domain proceedings involving
properties managed by Westco Equities. However, you have not identified any specific
decision. Generally speaking, unless Westco Equities, Westco Construction, or clients or
customers of these businesses have initiated the proceedings, are a named party in the
proceedings, or are the subject of the proceedings, your economic interests in these

5 Clients or customers of Westco Equities would include property owners with whom Westco
Equities has contracted to provide management services. Barring additional facts, you have no economic
interest in the tenants of the property owners. Likewise, a property owner who receives Section 8 rental
subsidies and who provides income to Westco Equities is a source of income to Westco Equities. As the
property manager with no ownership interest, you do not have economic interests in either HUD or the City
of Fresno Housing Authority.

File No. I-08-178
Page No. 7

business entities and sources of income are only indirectly involved in the governmental
decision.

However, an economic interest in a property owner as a source of income is
directly involved in an entiement domain proceeding involving the owner's property.
Furthermore, an economic interest in a property owner as a source of income is directly
involved in governmental decisions involving HUD or the City of Fresno Housing
Authority relating to low income housing, and decisions related to a redevelopment in
which the owner's property is located, if the proceedings involve the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract
with, the property owner. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(2).)

As for your economic interest in your personal finances, if facts suggest any
financial effect on your personal finances, your economic interest in your personal
finances is deemed to be directly involved in the governmental decision. (Regulation
18704.5.)

Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on your economic interests?

Materiality

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
agency's decision.

For economic interests in business entities directly involved in a decision,
including business entities which are a source of income to an official, the materiality
standard is given at Regulation 18705.1(b), which states, "the financial effects of a
government decision on a business entity which is directly involved in the government
decision is presumed to be material." This presumption of materiality may be rebutted
only by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have any financial effect on the business entity. Likewise, for economic interests in
persons who are sources of income to a public official directly involved in a decision,
Regulation 18705.3 states that "[a]ny reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person
who is a source of income to a public official, and who is directly involved . . . is deemed
material."

For economic interests in business entities indirectly involved in a decision,
including business entities that are a source of income to an official, the materiality
standard is given at Regulation 18705.1(c). The thresholds for materiality under this

File No. 1-08-178
Page No. 8

regulation vary with the size of the business. While you have not provided facts relating
to the size of Westco Equities or Westco Construction, Regulation 18705.1(c)(4)
provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on a business, not publicly
traded and relatively modest in economic size, is material if it is reasonably foreseeable
that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."

However, you should consult Regulation 18705.1(c) to ensure that you identify
the materiality threshold actually appropriate to your businesses.

Regulation 18705.3 provides the materiality standards for economic interests in
persons, other than business entities, who are sources of income to the public official and
are indirectly involved in a governmental decision. For an indirectly involved source of
income, who is an individual, the effect of a decision is material if any of the following
apply:

"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property)
by $1,000 or more; or

"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under [Regulation] 18705.2(b)."
(Regulation 18705.3(b)(3).)

In addition, a financial effect on any source of income, even an indirectly
involved source of income, is deemed material "if the public official receives or is
promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated,
aided, or hindered by the decision." (Regulation 18705.3(c).)

Effects on an official's personal finances are material as stated in Regulation
18705.5(a), which provides:

"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal
finances is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period. When

File No. I-08-178
Page No. 9

determining whether a governmental decision has a material financial
effect on a public official's economic interest in his or her personal
finances, neither a financial effect on the value of real property owned
directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on the gross
revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in
which the official has a direct or indirect investment interest shall be
considered."

Under Regulation 18705.5(a), a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses,
or value of assets and liabilities of either Westco Equities or Westco Construction would
not be considered a material financial effect on your economic interest in your personal
finances. However, any other financial effect on your personal finances, including those
of their immediate family, from a governmental decision is considered a material
financial effect if the decision will result in your or your family's personal expenses,
income, assets or liabilities increasing or decreasing by $250 or more in any 12-month
period. (Section 87103.)

Foreseeability

Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably
foreseeable" that the standards will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, supra,
at 198.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In
re Oglesby, supra, at 71), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public
official.

Steps Seven and Eight: Does the governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?

Even if you have a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects your economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).)

Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, you may be called upon to take part in
a decision despite the fact that you have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the

File No. I-08-178
Page No. 10


"legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in certain very
specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from acting.
(Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)

However, you have not presented any facts indicating that these exceptions are
applicable to your situation, so we will not address them further.

In closing, we hope that our explanation of the analytical process employed in
making these determinations, when coupled with your knowledge of the governmental
decisions that may come before the Fresno City Council or Fresno's redevelopment area,
will permit you to decide whether your involvement in the decisions will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests. We will be
happy to discuss particular problems with you, if you find that you need further
assistance in making this determination even after assembling the necessary factual
background.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

BGL:jgl

Enclosure

involving a managed property receiving federal rental subsidies, (2) eminent domain

You were elected to serve on the Fresno City Council for District Six and will be
taking office in January of 2009. As a councilmember, you will also be serving on the
board for Fresno's redevelopment agency.
In addition, you are the president of Westco Equities Inc., a privately held
Califomia corporation, and have a 60 percent ownership interest in this business. Westco
Equities provides property management services to residential and commercial properties
located in Central California. You also have a 60 percent ownership interest in Westco
Construction, [nc., which is a wholly owned subsidiary of Westco Equities. Westco
Construction provides construction services for properties, including those properties
managed by Westco Equities.
Some of the properties managed by Westco Equities are rented to low-income
renters who receive housing assistance in the form of rental subsidies through the Section
8 Rental Voucher Program, a federal housing program. Section 8 is administered
nationally by the United States Department of Housing and Urban Development
("HUD"), and administered locally by the City of Fresno Housing Authority. When
Westco Equities manages the properties, all forms of income, including rents, are placed
in a trust account, as required by the Department of Real Estate. All expenditures,
including payments for management services to Westco Equities, are paid through this

Westco Equities does not own any of the managed properties. Additionally, you

that receive Section 8 funds or that are located in redevelopment areas. Westco Equities
does not receive any other HUD funds other than those payments to Westco Equities
through the trust account as payments for management services.
sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's
economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has
adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an individual has a
disqualifying conflict of interest in a given govemmental decision, which we apply to
your question. (Regulation 18700(bX1)-(8).)
Step One: Are you a "public official?"
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(1).) A "public official" is "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency . . . ."
(Section 82048.) Upon taking office as a member of the Fresno City Council, you will be
a public official within the meaning of the Act.
Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.L) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the govemmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.) You will be making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision when acting in your role as a member of the city council.3
3 If a public official's office is listed in Section 81200 (*87200 filers" include members of a city
council) and he or she has a contlict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she
must: ( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in Regulation
18702.5(bX IXB), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room tbr the
duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests. special rules fbund in Regulation 18702.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)
Step Three: What are your "economic interests?"
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests. Of the economic interests recognized under the Act, those interests implicated
by your account of the facts are the following:
Real Property -- A public official has an economic interest in real property in
which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b);
Regulation 18703.2.)
Business Entity -- A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in
which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(a) and (d); Regulations 18703.1(a) and (b).)
Sources of Income -- A public official has an economic interest in any source of
income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months
prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) "Income" is defined to
include a pro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official
(or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a lO-percent or greater
interest. (Section 82030(a).)
Personal Financial Effects -- A public official has an economic interest in his or
her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is known as
the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)
Properties Managed by Westco Equities:
Section 82033 of the Act defines "interest in real property" and provides:
"'Interest in real property' includes any leasehold, beneficial or ownership
interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property located in
the jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public
official, or other filer, or his or her immediate family if the fair market
value of the interest is two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. Interests in
real property of an individual includes a pro rata share of interests in real
property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or
immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially. a 1O-percent
interest or greater."
While you have a 60 percent ownership interest in Westco Equities and its wholly
owned subsidiary Westco Constmction, you have stated that Westco Equities does not
own any of the properties it manages. Moreover, the mere fact that Westco Equities acts
as a manager foi a property does not give rise to an economic interest in real property.a
Westco Equities:
You have a 60 percent ownership interest in Westco Equities. From the facts
presented, it appears that you have an investment of $2,000 or more in Westco Equities
and are a director, officer, partner, trLlstee, employee, or manager of this business.
Therefore, you have an economic interest in Westco Equities as a business entity.
(Section 87103 (a) and (d).) Moreover, if you have received income of $500 or more
from Westco Equities in the 12 months prior to a governmental decision, you also have
an economic interest in this business as a source of income. (Section 87102(c).)
Westco Construction:
Westco Construction is a wholly owned subsidiary of Westco Equities. Under
Regulation 18703.1(c) and 18703.3(a)(2), an official also has an economic interest in any
business entity which is a parent or subsidiary of, or is otherwise related to, a business in
which the official has an economic interest as either a business entity or a source of
income. Because Westco Construction is a wholly owned subsidiary of Westco Equities,
you also have an economic interest in Westco Construction under both Regulation
18703.1(c) and 18703.3(aX2). (See Section 18703.1(d) for definition of "parent-
subsidiary relationship" and "otherwise related business entity.")
Clients or Customers of Westco Equities and Westco Construction:
You have a 60 percent interest in both Westco Equities and Westco Construction.
Thus, you have an economic interest in any client or customer of these businesses if your
pro rata share of the income derived from the client or customer aggregates to $500 or
more in the 12 months prior to the governmental decision. (Section 82030(a).) However,
you have not identified any particular clients or customers of Westco Equities and
Westco Construction who may qualify as sources of income. We therefore cannot
analyze any potential conflicts of interest you may have in decisions affecting these
clients or customers. You will still need to determine whether any of the clients or
customers of Westco Equities or Westco Construction qualify as a source of income and
whether a conflict of interest will arise based upon an economic interest in one of these
n Because this letter is based upon the facts presented, our analysis of your potential economic
interests in real property is limited to those properties managed by Westco Equities. If you have other
interests in real property that may be atfected by a governmental decision, those interests must also be
considered prior to making, participating in, ur influencing the decision. If you need additional assistance
related to other interests in real property. it is advisable that you seek further advice providing all relevant
intbrmation.
clients or customers prior to making, participating in, or influencing any decision that
may affect the client or customer.)
Personal Finances:
A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances.
A governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interestlf the decision will
result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her
immediate family increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)
Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a business entity or source of income is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency when that business entity or source of
income, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the su6'ject
of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, reniwal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlemenf to, or
contract with, the subject person."
Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in
governmental decisions under the rules quoted above are regarded, as tndirecr./y involved.
(Regulation 1 8704. I (b).)
You have asked whether you may participate in general discussions related to the
following: (l) decisions involving HUD or the City of Fiesno Housing Authority relating
to low income housing, (2) decisions affecting a redevelopment area in which properties
managed by Westco Equities are located, and (3) eminent domain proceedings involving
properties managed by Westco Equities. However, you have not identified iny specific
decision. Generally speaking, unless Westco Equities, Westco Construction, or clients or
customers of these businesses have initiated the proceedings, are a named party in the
proceedings, or are the subject of the proceedings, your economic interests in these
s Clients or customers of Westco Equities would include property owners with whom Westco
Equities has contracted to provide management services. Barring adoitionat tacts, you have no economic
interest in the tenants of the property owners. Likewise, a prop.ity owner who receives Section g rental
subsidies and who provides income to Westco Equities is a source of income to Westco Equities. As the
property manager with no trwnership interest. yuu do not have economic interests in either HUD or the City
of Fresno Housing Authority.
business entities and sources of income are only indirectly involved in the governmental
decision.
However, an economic interest in a property owner as a source of income is
directly involved in an eminent domain proceeding involving the owner's property.
Furthermore, an economic interest in a property owner as a source of income is directly
involved in govemmental decisions involving HUD or the City of Fresno Housing
Authority relating to low income housing, and decisions related to a redevelopment in
which the owner's property is located, if the proceedings involve the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract
with, the property owner. (Regulation 1870a.1(a)(2).)
As for your economic interest in your personal finances, if facts suggest any
financial effect on your personal finances, your economic interest in your personal
finances is deemed to be directly involved in the governmental decision. (Regulation
r8704.5.)
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on your economic interests?
Materiality
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
govemmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
agency's decision.
For economic interests in business entities directly involved in a decision,
including business entities which are a source of income to an official, the materiality
standard is given at Regulation 18705.1(b), which states, "the financial effects of a
government decision on a business entity which is directly involved in the govemment
decision is presumed to be material." This presumption of materiality may be rebutted
only by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have any financial effect on the business entity. Likewise, for economic interests in
persons who are sources of income to a public official directly involved in a decision,
Regulation 18705.3 states that "[a]ny reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person
who is a source of income to a public official, and who is directly involved . . . is deemed
material."
For economic interests in business entities indirectly involved in a decision,
including business entities that are a source of income to an official, the materiality
standard is given at Regulation 18705.1(c). The thresholds for materiality under this
regulation vary with the size of the business. While you have not provided facts relating
to the size of Westco Equities or Westco Construction, Regulation 18705.1(c)(a)
provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on a business, not publicly
traded and relatively modest in economic size, is material if it is reasonably foreseeable
that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(c) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."
However, you should consult Regulation 18705.1(c) to ensure that you identify
the materiality threshold actually appropriate to your businesses.
Regulation 18705.3 provides the materiality standards for economic interests in
persons, other than business entities, who are sources of income to the public official and
are indirecrly involved in a govemmental decision. For an indirectly involved source of
income, who is an individual, the effect of a decision is material if any of the following
apply:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property)
by $1,000 or more; or
"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under [Regulation] 18705.2(b).',
(Regulation 1 8705.3(bX3 ).)
ln addition, a financial effect on any source of income, even an indirectly
involved source of income, is deemed material "if the public official receives or is
promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated,
aided, or hindered by the decision." (Regulation 18705.3(c).)
Effects on an official's personal finances are material as stated in Regulation
I 8705.5(a), which provides:
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal
finances is material if it is at least $250 in any l2-month period. when
determining whether a govemmental decision has a material financial
effect on a public official's economic interest in his or her personal
finances, neither a financial effect on the value of real property owned
directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on the gross
revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in
which the official has a direct or indirect investment interest shall be
considered."
Under Regulation 18705.5(a), a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses,
or value of assets and liabilities of either Westco Equities or Westco Construction would
not be considered a material financial effect on your economic interest in your personal
finances. However, any other financial effect on your personal finances, including those
of their immediate family, from a govemmental decision is considered a material
financial effect if the decision will result in your or your family's personal expenses,
income, assets or liabilities increasing or decreasing by $250 or more in any l2-month
period. (Section 87103.)
Foreseeability
Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably
foreseeable" that the standards will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the govemmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time h decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, supra,
at 198.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In
re Oglesby, supra, at 7l), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is i
determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public
official.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does the governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
Even if you have a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects your economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87 103; Regulation 18707(a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, yoll may be called upon to take part in
a decision despite the fact that you have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the
"legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in certain very
specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from acting.
(Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)
However, you have not presented any facts indicating that these exceptions are
applicable to your situation, so we will not address them further.
In closing, we hope that our explanation of the analyical process employed in
making these determinations, when coupled with your knowledge of the governmental
decisions that may come before the Fresno City Council or Fresno's redevelopment area,
will permit you to decide whether your involvement in the decisions will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests. We will be
happy to discuss particular problems with you, if you find that you need further
assistance in making this determination even after assembling the necessary factual
background.

QUESTION: As a city councilmember and the majority owner of a property management
business, may you make, participate in making, or influence governmental decisions
involving a managed property receiving federal rental subsidies, (2) eminent domain
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 I 000 through 9 I 0 14. unless otherwise indicated.
I Intbrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opini<ln or
tirrmal written advice. (Section 831 l4: Regulation l8-ll9(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Because your question seeks general
guidance and is not limited to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your
reqllest as one for informal assistance.2 Nothing in this letter should be construed to
evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained
in this letter apply only to prospective actions. [n addition, this letter is based on the facts
presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a

File No. t-08-178
proceedings related to a managed property, or (3) a redevelopment area in which a
managed property is located?

CONCLUSION: Because we do not have sufficient information to determine whether a particular
governmental decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
your economic interests, we are unable to determine whether you have a conflict of
interest in these decisions. However, based on the facts you have provided, you may not
make, participate in making, or influence decisions that have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on Westco Equities, Westco Construction, certain clients or
customers of these businesses, or your personal finances.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 November 19, 2008 Lee Brand Councilmember Elect for District Six City of Fresno 2600 Fresno Street, Room 2031 Fresno, CA 93721-3602 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-08-178 Dear Mr. Brand: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your question seeks general guidance and is not limited to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTION As a city councilmember and the majority owner of a property management business, may you make, participate in making, or influence governmental decisions involving a managed property receiving federal rental subsidies, (2) eminent domain ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.) File No. I-08-178 Page No. 2 proceedings related to a managed property, or (3) a redevelopment area in which a managed property is located? CONCLUSION Because we do not have sufficient information to determine whether a particular governmental decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests, we are unable to determine whether you have a conflict of interest in these decisions. However, based on the facts you have provided, you may not make, participate in making, or influence decisions that have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Westco Equities, Westco Construction, certain clients or customers of these businesses, or your personal finances. FACTS You were elected to serve on the Fresno City Council for District Six and will be taking office in January of 2009. As a councilmember, you will also be serving on the board for Fresno's redevelopment agency. In addition, you are the president of Westco Equities Inc., a privately held California corporation, and have a 60 percent ownership interest
